title: The cleaner who knew the building best
summary: Argue that the office cleaner, who moves through every floor at night, often understands an organisation better than its managers, and that we wrongly rank such knowledge as worthless.
topic: Does the person who cleans an office often understand it better than the people who run it?
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) arguing that an office cleaner, who quietly passes through every room, can understand an organisation more truthfully than senior managers do — and that we are wrong to treat such 'low' work and its knowledge as worthless. Use concrete observations, not slogans. Use present perfect at least once, a conditional at least once, and 'although' or 'however' at least once.
hintStarters: Long after the managers have gone home, one person walks through every office on every floor, and what she sees…
Although her job is ranked near the bottom of the pay scale, the picture she has of the company is…
If you wanted an honest answer about how a workplace really runs, you would do better to ask…

gạt bỏ kiến thức của ai đó

hintVocab.phrase: to read the mood of a place
hintVocab.meaningVi: đọc được bầu không khí của một nơi

một góc quan sát thuận lợi
địa vị
xem sự hiện diện của ai đó là điều hiển nhiên
sự thật ở cấp cơ sở

sampleText:
Every weekday at half past six, when the last manager has finally left, Rosa starts on the fourth floor and works her way down. She empties the bins, wipes the desks, and pushes her cart along corridors that, an hour earlier, were full of people who barely looked at her. By midnight she has been inside every room in the building: the open-plan floors, the locked director's office she has a key to, the meeting rooms with the whiteboards no one rubs clean. On paper, Rosa is at the very bottom of the company. In practice, she may understand it better than anyone on the top floor.

Consider what the bins alone tell her. She knows which team has been ordering takeaway at eleven at night for three weeks, because the project is in trouble. She knows which department has stopped throwing away celebration cake and started throwing away job-search printouts. She has noticed that the director's bin is mostly empty, because real decisions are made elsewhere, in rooms where the bin is always full. None of this appears in any report the managers read. They see the company through spreadsheets and meetings, which show them what people choose to present. Rosa sees what people leave behind when they think no one is watching, and what people leave behind is usually more honest than what they say. If you genuinely wanted to know whether a workplace was healthy, you would learn more from a week of emptying its bins than from a month of its official updates.

Although Rosa's job sits near the bottom of every pay scale and status ladder we have invented, the knowledge it gives her is not low at all. We have made a strange mistake: we assume that the importance of work and the visibility of work are the same thing, and they are not. The building does not function because of the people who are photographed for the annual report; it functions because someone keeps it clean, and because that someone pays a quiet, continuous attention that the busy and the important have no time for. I am not romanticising the work — it is hard, underpaid, and too often invisible, and it should be none of those things. But the next time someone speaks of 'unskilled' labour, I think of Rosa walking her silent floors at midnight, holding in her head a truer map of the organisation than the people who run it will ever possess. Her work is not beneath our notice. Our failure to notice it is beneath us.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'by [time]' for accumulated experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'By midnight she has been inside every room in the building' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'by + thời điểm' để diễn tả một trải nghiệm đã tích lũy hoàn tất tính đến thời điểm đó. Nó nhấn mạnh phạm vi của những gì cô đã chứng kiến.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'By midnight she has been inside every room in the building' uses the present perfect with 'by + a point in time' to describe accumulated experience complete by that point. It stresses the scope of what she has witnessed.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional to recommend a better source of truth
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you genuinely wanted to know whether a workplace was healthy, you would learn more from a week of emptying its bins…' dùng câu điều kiện loại 2 (if + past simple, would + V) để đưa ra một lời khuyên giả định và đầy sức thuyết phục, mời người đọc cân nhắc một góc nhìn bất ngờ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you genuinely wanted to know whether a workplace was healthy, you would learn more from a week of emptying its bins…' uses the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base verb) to make a hypothetical, persuasive recommendation, inviting the reader to consider a surprising viewpoint.

sampleTranslationVi:
Mỗi ngày trong tuần vào lúc sáu giờ rưỡi, khi người quản lý cuối cùng cuối cùng đã rời đi, Rosa bắt đầu ở tầng bốn và làm dần xuống. Cô đổ rác, lau bàn, và đẩy chiếc xe đẩy của mình dọc những hành lang mà một giờ trước còn đầy người gần như không thèm nhìn cô. Đến nửa đêm cô đã vào bên trong mọi căn phòng trong tòa nhà: những tầng văn phòng mở, văn phòng giám đốc bị khóa mà cô có chìa, những phòng họp với các bảng trắng không ai lau sạch. Trên giấy tờ, Rosa ở dưới đáy của công ty. Trên thực tế, cô có thể hiểu nó rõ hơn bất kỳ ai trên tầng cao nhất.

Hãy xét xem chỉ riêng những thùng rác đã nói cho cô điều gì. Cô biết đội nào đã đặt đồ ăn mang về lúc mười một giờ đêm suốt ba tuần, bởi vì dự án đang gặp rắc rối. Cô biết phòng ban nào đã ngừng vứt đi bánh ăn mừng và bắt đầu vứt đi những bản in tìm việc làm. Cô đã nhận ra rằng thùng rác của giám đốc gần như trống rỗng, bởi vì những quyết định thực sự được đưa ra ở nơi khác, trong những căn phòng mà thùng rác luôn đầy. Không điều nào trong số này xuất hiện trong bất kỳ báo cáo nào các nhà quản lý đọc. Họ nhìn công ty qua các bảng tính và các cuộc họp, những thứ cho họ thấy điều mà người ta chọn để trình bày. Rosa nhìn thấy những gì người ta để lại khi họ nghĩ không ai đang nhìn, và những gì người ta để lại thường trung thực hơn những gì họ nói. Nếu bạn thực sự muốn biết liệu một nơi làm việc có lành mạnh hay không, bạn sẽ học được nhiều hơn từ một tuần đổ rác của nó so với một tháng các bản cập nhật chính thức.

Mặc dù công việc của Rosa nằm gần đáy của mọi thang lương và nấc thang địa vị mà chúng ta đã nghĩ ra, kiến thức mà nó mang lại cho cô hoàn toàn không hề thấp kém. Chúng ta đã phạm một sai lầm kỳ lạ: chúng ta cho rằng tầm quan trọng của công việc và mức độ dễ thấy của công việc là cùng một thứ, mà chúng thì không. Tòa nhà vận hành không phải nhờ những người được chụp ảnh cho báo cáo thường niên; nó vận hành được vì có ai đó giữ cho nó sạch sẽ, và vì người ấy dành một sự chú tâm lặng lẽ, liên tục mà những người bận rộn và quan trọng không có thời gian dành cho. Tôi không lãng mạn hóa công việc này — nó vất vả, lương thấp, và quá thường xuyên vô hình, và lẽ ra nó không nên là bất kỳ điều nào trong số đó. Nhưng lần tới khi ai đó nói về lao động 'không có kỹ năng', tôi nghĩ đến Rosa đi qua những tầng lầu lặng lẽ của mình lúc nửa đêm, giữ trong đầu một tấm bản đồ chân thực hơn về tổ chức so với những người điều hành nó sẽ bao giờ có được. Công việc của cô không phải thứ dưới tầm chú ý của chúng ta. Việc chúng ta không nhận ra nó mới là điều dưới tầm của chúng ta.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect with 'by [time]' to describe accumulated experience?
options: By midnight she has being inside every room.
By midnight she had been inside every room until now.
By midnight she has been inside every room.
By midnight she is been inside every room.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has been' is the correct present perfect. Option 2 uses 'being' instead of 'been'. Option 3 mixes past perfect with 'until now'. Option 4 uses 'is been' instead of 'has been'.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect = has + been; 'being' and 'is been' are wrong here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a second conditional correctly to make a recommendation?
options: If you wanted the truth, you will learn more from the bins than from the reports.
If you would want the truth, you would learn more from the bins than from the reports.
If you want the truth, you would learn more from the bins than from the reports.
If you wanted the truth, you would learn more from the bins than from the reports.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional = if + past simple, would + base verb. Option 2 uses 'will'. Option 3 puts 'would' in the if-clause. Option 4 mixes present simple with 'would'.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional: if + past simple, then would + base verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede the cleaner's low pay rank?
options: Although her job sits near the bottom of the pay scale, the knowledge it gives her is not low.
Although her job sits near the bottom of the pay scale, but the knowledge it gives her is not low.
Although her job sits near the bottom of the pay scale the knowledge it gives her is not low.
Her job sits near the bottom of the pay scale, although however the knowledge it gives her is not low.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, + main clause' is correct. Option 2 adds 'but'. Option 3 omits the comma. Option 4 stacks 'although' and 'however'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' stands alone with a comma; never pair it with 'but' or 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally describes ignoring or undervaluing someone?
options: People hardly looked on her.
People barely looked at her.
People scarce looked at her.
People rarely looked over her.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Barely looked at' is natural and idiomatic. Option 2 uses 'looked on', which changes the meaning. Option 3 uses 'scarce' as an adverb, which is archaic. Option 4 uses 'looked over', meaning to inspect, not the intended sense.
mcQuestions.hint: We 'look at' a person; choose the most natural near-zero adverb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly contrasts importance and visibility?
options: The importance of work and the visibility of work are not a same thing.
The importance of work and the visibility of work is not the same thing.
The importance of work and the visibility of work are not the same thing.
The importance of work and the visibility of work are not the same things.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option 1 is correct: plural subject 'are', and the fixed phrase 'the same thing'. Option 2 says 'a same thing', which is ungrammatical. Option 3 uses singular 'is' with a plural subject. Option 4 says 'the same things', which clashes with the singular idea being compared.
mcQuestions.hint: Two subjects take 'are'; the idiom is 'the same thing' (singular).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'have no time for' to describe the busy and important?
options: The busy and the important have no time of it.
The busy and the important have not time for it.
The busy and the important have no time to it.
The busy and the important have no time for it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have no time for + noun' is the correct idiom. Option 2 uses 'of'. Option 3 uses 'have not time', which is archaic/ungrammatical here. Option 4 uses 'to it', the wrong preposition.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase is 'have no time for' something.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'assume that' to state a mistaken belief?
options: We assume that importance and visibility are the same.
We assume importance and visibility being the same.
We assume that importance and visibility to be the same.
We assume of importance and visibility are the same.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Assume that + clause' is correct. Option 2 uses '-ing' where a clause is needed. Option 3 uses 'to be' incorrectly after 'that'. Option 4 inserts a stray 'of'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'assume that' plus a full clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes work done out of public view?
options: Much of the real work happens behind the scene.
Much of the real work happens behind the scenes.
Much of the real work happens behind of the scenes.
Much of the real work happens behind the scenery.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Behind the scenes' (plural) is the fixed idiom. Option 2 uses the singular 'scene'. Option 3 adds a stray 'of'. Option 4 uses 'scenery', which means landscape or stage decoration.
mcQuestions.hint: The idiom is plural: 'behind the scenes'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'more ... than' to compare two sources of insight?
options: You would learn more from the bins as from the reports.
You would learn more from the bins then from the reports.
You would learn more from the bins than from the reports.
You would learn most from the bins than from the reports.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'More ... than' is the correct comparative structure. Option 2 uses 'as' instead of 'than'. Option 3 uses 'then' (a time word) instead of 'than'. Option 4 uses the superlative 'most' with 'than'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best delivers the article's closing point clearly and naturally?
options: Her work is not beneath our notice, our failure to notice it is beneath us.
Her work is not beneath our notice; our failure to noticing it is beneath us.
Her work is not beneath of our notice; our failure to notice it is beneath us.
Her work is not beneath our notice; our failure to notice it is beneath us.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option 1 uses a semicolon to join two balanced clauses and the infinitive 'to notice'. Option 2 is a comma splice. Option 3 uses 'to noticing'. Option 4 adds a stray 'of' after 'beneath'.
mcQuestions.hint: Join the two contrasting clauses with a semicolon; use 'to notice' and drop any 'of'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says we wrongly assume that the importance of work and the visibility of work are the same thing. Think of a role in your own community that is essential but largely unseen. Why do you think pay and respect so often fail to follow real importance, and what would it take to change that?
